issue_title: why the Validation-accuracy drops so much when I use  mx.mod.BucketingModule() to define a symbol?
Load pretrained model with new load_parameters() API
issue_body:
## Description
Cannot load only convolutional layers with pre-trained model using ```load_parameters()``` API 

## Environment info (Required)
Ubuntu 16.04
mxnet-cu90 1.3.0b20180821

## Error Message:
missing_parameters

## Minimum reproducible example
```
from mxnet.gluon.model_zoo.vision import get_model

# download the pretrained model with the path: model_path
model = get_model('resnet101_v2')
model.features.load_parameters(model_path)
```

## What have you tried to solve it?
The reason of this issue is load_parameters called from ```features block```, in which name of parameters starts with numbers. But the pretrained model parameters starts with ```features```.
The ```load_parameters``` cannot find the corresponding name in this case.

One way to work around this issue is using the ```model.save_params()``` to save again and load using the API ```load_parameters```. However, the save_params() is deprecated.

To prevent any problem with loading parameters, I propose this issue and hope the team can fix it.

issue_title: [QA] why not all examples run out of the box?
issue_body:
In the last three months, i have tried several examples in R or python, with a number of versions of mxnet. Very often, the examples throw errors of one kind or another when you run them 'as is'.

Do you think it would be possible to make sure all examples run before accepting a PR? 
In fact, why not use the various examples as test cases where results could be checked against expected values (using fixed seeds)?

issue_title: Does mxnet provide Model Average for distributed training?
issue_body:
Now I would like to train the example code _train_cifar10_resnet.py_ with two local workers, is there any existed model average implementation I can directly use? (For example, CNTK has this kind of feature)
Or should I write the corresponding update rules for the kv-store?

Thanks a lot!

issue_title: RFC: Add "capabilities" section to version command output
issue_body:
Keeping in mind our [versioning and compatibility scheme](http://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/compatibility.html), clients are better off interrogating a set of capabilities rather than performing version number checks.

I'm proposing that we augment the version command so that it outputs something like this:

```
{
    "version": "3.5.0",
    "capabilities": ["relative-roots"]
}
```

Including the capabilities list in the version command allows clients to query the version number and obtain the capability set in a single round trip.  If we were to add a separate command just for capabilities, clients would need to query the version number to determine if they could then query for capabilities.

As part of this we should define the capabilities.  I'm inclined to just have `relative-roots` as the first and only listed capability initially.  All other functionality is effectively and implicitly available if the `capabilities` field is present in the `version` output.

I'd like to define `relative-roots` as complete support for `watch-project` with the `relative_root` parameter for the query, trigger and subscription subsystems.

An additional command that we can add would be to help check compatibility, so for example, the client would do:

```
["capabilities", {"required": ["relative-roots"], "optional": ["pcre"]}]
```

The response would look something like:

```
{
   "version": "3.7.0",
   "capabilities": {
       "relative-roots": true,
       "pcre": false
   }
}
```

If any of the capability names in the `required` field are missing, the `capabilities` command would add an error field listing the missing capabilities, causing the command to error out.  The `capabilities` map has boolean values that indicate whether a given capability is supported.

Thoughts?

issue_title: How to detect presence or absence of a sequence carrying a deletion
issue_body:
Dear Martin,

Thank you so much again for explaining ARIBA 
during the conference ABPHM2017 in Sanger in May.

I do think ARIBA is a really nice, well-designed, 
generally useful tool.

I'm trying to detect presence or absence of a promoter
sequence carrying a deletion associated with AMR.

When I used the sequence carrying the deletion as a reference,
a report of ARIBA run for a sample without carrying the deletion is
http://yahara.hustle.ne.jp/projects/ariba/report.tsv
in which column T (ref_ctg_effect) is correctly indicated as "INS".

However, ARIBA summary for the report 
http://yahara.hustle.ne.jp/projects/ariba/out.summary.csv
says "mtrR_Adel_promoter.match" is yes.

But this sample does not have the deletion, so I would like to 
have a summary in which "mtrR_Adel_promoter.match" is no.

Of course I read definition of "match" judged by the summary command
https://github.com/sanger-pathogens/ariba/wiki/Task:-summary

Is ARIBA not designed to detect this kind of deletion?
I appreciate your comment.

Best wishes,

Koji

***
Koji Yahara
Senior Research Fellow
Antimicrobial Resistance Research Center
National Institute of Infectious Diseases
4-2-1 Aobacho, Higashimurayama, Tokyo
189-0002 Japan

issue_title: Use a 96-bit nonce for the IV
issue_body:
Currently your code uses a 256-bit random IV (nonce) for AES-GCM. GCM is designed to use a 96-bit nonce and if you give it a longer (or shorter) value it hashes it to obtain a value of the correct length (see Algorithm 4 of [NIST SP.800-38D](http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/Legacy/SP/nistspecialpublication800-38d.pdf). As you are using random IVs anyway, this doesn't change much from a security perspective, but it is inefficient to use a 256-bit value here when 96-bits is all that is required (after base64-encoding you are adding about an extra 27 bytes to each encrypted value).

Note that the IV for GCM does not need to be random, it just needs to never repeat for the same key (a nonce: see the Deterministic IV Construction in Section 8.2.1 of the linked specification). As you are generating fresh random 256-bit AES keys for each file and processing the whole file at once, I would suggest just keeping a simple counter that you increment for each value that you encrypt in the file: 0 for the first, 1 for the second etc. Encode this as a 96-bit value (e.g. big-endian) and use that as the nonce. That way you do not need to explicitly record the IV at all.

issue_body:
After having set up sops and gpg to have a play with sops, I find that the examples for the --set and --extract flags do not work as they stand.

**For `--extract`, the examples in README.rst are:**

`sops -d ~/git/svc/sops/example.yaml --extract '["app2"]["key"]'`
and
`sops -d ~/git/svc/sops/example.yaml --extract '["an_array"][1]'`

For me, neither of these will print only the desired key, but rather the whole file is decrypted and printed to stdout.  I did get it to work in a different parameter order though, like so the single key value is output.

`sops -d --extract '["app2"]["key"]' ~/git/svc/sops/example.yaml`
and
`sops -d --extract '["an_array"][1]' ~/git/svc/sops/example.yaml`

Not sure if this is a simple documentation issue, or not "working as desired".

**For `--set`, the examples in README.rst are:**

`sops ~/git/svc/sops/example.yaml --set '["app2"]["key"]' '"app2keystringvalue"'`
`sops ~/git/svc/sops/example.yaml --set '["an_array"][1]' '"secretuser2"'`
and
`sops ~/git/svc/sops/example.yaml --set '["an_array"][1]' '{"uid1":null,"uid2":1000,"uid3":["bob"]}'`

For me, none of these work as described. Instead, the entire file is decrypted and opened in `vim` (my configured `$EDITOR`).  Here, unlike for `--extract`, I did not find any alternative order of params that works.

(If it helps, I'm on MacOS High Sierra, `gpg --version` "gpg (GnuPG) 2.2.4 libgcrypt 1.8.2", `sops -v` "sops 3.0.0")

issue_title: Rank beam_width ctc_beam_search_decoder() results on language model
issue_body:
Instead of relying on a fix of tensorflow/tensorflow#6034 simply obtain `beam_width` results from `ctc_beam_search_decoder()` and rank them based on the current language model.

This has the advantage not not relying upon the slow Norvig spelling corrector[[1](http://norvig.com/spell-correct.html)]. However, it has the disadvantage of likely spelling worse than including the Norvig spelling corrector[[1](http://norvig.com/spell-correct.html)].

This could should be implemented in a branch, then a WER test should be done comparing it to the current language model + Norvig spelling corrector[[1](http://norvig.com/spell-correct.html)]. Whichever has the lowest WER remains in the main branch.

issue_title: Support prompt parameter none instead of the delegation endpoint
issue_body:
prompt=none is the OIDC standard and is now supported by auth0:
https://auth0.com/docs/api-auth/tutorials/silent-authentication

Sample (Lua):
https://github.com/pingidentity/lua-resty-openidc/commit/2d21cb521f5edebb76af27bfb1f7a44264d8372e

In other words, instead of calling the delegation endpoint (auth0 specific) we pass a prompt=none parameter to the authorization endpoint, which will return the current id_token (just like delegation endpoint) without prompting the user

issue_title: Generate natural text from an EDTFObject
issue_body:
EDTFObjects only return their EDTF string representation, which isn't the best format to show to people not familiar with the format.

The current recommended approach is to generate EDTF from natural text, and store both. It would be nice if you could derive a basic natural language representation from the EDTF object.
For details see https://github.com/nigelsmall/py2neo/issues/484

issue_title: FBX importer crashes with zlib compiled with Z_SOLO
issue_body: If using an external zlib compiled with Z_SOLO, the FBX importer will crash decompressing a gzipped stream because it doesn't check the [return value of inflateInit](https://github.com/assimp/assimp/blob/master/code/FBXParser.cpp#L552), which will fail with Z_SOLO defined and zstream.zalloc not set to a memory allocator.

issue_title: Configparser does not respect order of config files with includes
issue_body:
When you have an include statement in the user `.gitconfig` there content will take precedence over the local `.git/config` of the repository.

E.g. the following code:
`entry = repo.config_reader().get_value('gitlab', 'url')`

With the following config setup:

`~/.gitconfig`:
```ini
[include]
    path = ~/.gitconfig-local
[gitlab]
    url = url1
```

`~/.gitconfig-local`:
```ini
[gitlab]
    url = url2
```

`.git/config`:
```ini
[gitlab]
    url = url3
```

Will return `url2` I would expect to receive `url3` instead as local config should take precedence as the include was in a more global config.

Looks like the issue resides inside the `read ` function e.g. https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/c823d482d03caa8238b48714af4dec6d9e476520/git/config.py#L427 will just add the includes to the list, which is just wrong, as they should be read instantly to overwrite just the already known configs.

As the function is just to big, I'm not able to provide a fix.

Versions in use:
```
>>> import git; git.__version__
'2.1.3'
```

```
python --version
Python 2.7.13
```

issue_title: Execute Command doesnt work with dropbear
issue_body:
Environment:
    SSHLibrary version: 2.1.1
    Paramiko version: 1.15.1
    Used interpreter: Python 2.7.6
    Operating system: Ubuntu 14.04.1 LTS

Since upgrading dropbear in my embedded target (to 2014.66) Executing Command or rather waiting for the Command Result leads to hanging in an endless loop in read_outputs in paramikos recv_exit_status() function. It seems that upon receiving dropbears EOF  we never send a EOF which leads to the connection still being open. SSHLibraries _shell_open() function however makes us think the connection was teared down correctly.

I have tracked down the issue in dropbear to their changeset:

936:d93a6bcf616f
Improve handling lots of concurrent forwarded connections. Increase connection backlog, avoid check_close() for channels that haven't had IO

but I cant actually change that code in our target. (all other ssh connections do work without problems).

So below is a patch that fixes my problem by checking if exit_status_ready() will be successful before actually exexuting recv_exit_status(). In case it's not, the write direction is closed forcefully (which sends out the missing? EOF).

It works for me but I'm not sure if this is ok for everybody...? Any experts on this?



Index: SSHLibrary/src/SSHLibrary/pythonclient.py
===================================================================
--- SSHLibrary.orig/src/SSHLibrary/pythonclient.py	2014-12-15 11:38:59.830379909 +0100
+++ SSHLibrary/src/SSHLibrary/pythonclient.py	2014-12-15 11:40:13.132928618 +0100
@@ -169,6 +169,8 @@
 
     def read_outputs(self):
         stderr, stdout = self._receive_stdout_and_stderr()
+        if not self._shell.exit_status_ready():
+            self._shell.shutdown_write()
         rc = self._shell.recv_exit_status()
         self._shell.close()
         return stdout, stderr, rc

issue_title: Restrictions for type of uncertainty in nix and odML
issue_body:
Currently the only types accepted for uncertainties are integer and float in Nix. In odML however, there is no restriction when generating an odML document, but for documents loaded from disc all uncertainties have the type string.
I am not sure if it makes sense to also allow string uncertainties in Nix or if odML uncertainties should also be restricted to integer and float. One example for needing a string as uncertainty would be to store something like '2 standard deviations' but I am not sure if this is a real use case.
Any opinions on that?

In applying the `@cligj.features_in_arg` [to the mapbox cli](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-cli-py/pull/34#issue-122948259), there were a few cases where we needed some slight variants. Specifically, we want to support single-feature arguments, and single/multi-feature options. So

* `@cligj.feature_in_arg` (singular)
* `@cligj.feature_in_opt` 
* `@cligj.features_in_opt`

Naming variants might be nice.

Supporting additional callbacks for user-defined validation functions has also crossed my mind. But I'm leaning towards encouraging that level of validation to go in the processing function itself.

**Do we want a decorator constructor?** something like
```
def features_input(type='arg', name='waypoints', validate_func=None):
    ....
    return decorator
```

In closed issues I've found similar reports, but it seems that subsequent updates—I'm using v0.3.1—haven't dealt with the problem. I'm occasionally getting error messages (see screenshot below); in this case mapbox-cli-py is part of a shell script that runs within BitBar.

issue_title: Error installing pipenv on Windows Subsystem for Linux
issue_body:
Trying to install pipenv on Ubuntu > [WSL](http://wsl-guide.org/en/latest/)
Followed [instructions](https://docs.pipenv.org/#install-pipenv-today) from docs:
```
$ sudo apt install software-properties-common python-software-properties
[ OK ]
$ sudo add-apt-repository ppa:pypa/ppa
[ OK ]
$ sudo apt update
...
```

`sudo apt update` fails with the message below:
```
Err:5 http://ppa.launchpad.net/pypa/ppa/ubuntu xenial Release
404  Not Found
```

Since it failed to update the repo, installs also fails:
```
$ sudo apt install pipenv
E: Unable to locate package pipenv
```


I am not very familiar with ubuntu's packaging repositories,
but are missing a release compatible with WSL ubuntu flavor (xenial) ?

```lsb_release -a```

```No LSB modules are available.
Distributor ID: Ubuntu
Description:    Ubuntu 16.04.3 LTS
Release:        16.04
Codename:       xenial
```

I have an odd issue, and I'm not sure if I'm doing something wrong since I just starting using pipenv, but something seems off with the sub-dependency checking. 

With a brand new project, if I do `pipenv install channels==1.1.8`, I get the following output:

```
Successfully installed Django-2.0.3 asgiref-1.1.2 channels-1.1.8 daphne-1.4.2
```

That is what I would expect. But the lock file has version `asgiref==2.2.0`, and `daphne==2.1.0`. Which are obviously different versions than pip actually installed.

If I then then try to run `pipenv install asgiref==1.1.2`, I get the dependency resolution error:

```
Warning: Your dependencies could not be resolved. You likely have a mismatch in your sub-dependencies.
  You can use $ pipenv install --skip-lock to bypass this mechanism, then run $ pipenv graph to inspect the situation.
Could not find a version that matches asgiref==1.1.2,~=2.1
```

If I run with verbose (`pipenv install asgiref==1.1.2 -v`), it tells me `channels 1.1.8` requires `asgiref~=2.1`:

```
channels==1.1.8           requires asgiref~=2.1, daphne~=2.0, Django>=1.11
```

Which is incorrect according to: https://github.com/django/channels/blob/1.1.8/setup.py#L16

issue_title: Consider adding a single command to completely synchronize everything with the Pipfile
issue_body:
Say I manually edit my Pipfile -- add some dependencies, remove some dependencies. I would expect that there would be a single command for updating both the Pipfile.lock and the virtual environment to reflect the current state of the Pipfile. As far as I can tell, there isn't.

----
Here's what there is instead (along with some critiques):

**lock**: updates the Pipfile.lock to reflect the current state of the Pipfile

**sync**: installs all packages specified in the Pipfile.lock, but does not uninstall packages *not* specified in Pipfile.lock (which makes the name misleading, since it doesn't entirely synchronize the environment with the Pipfile.lock)

**update**: runs **lock**, then **sync**, i.e. it does not uninstall packages *not* specified in Pipfile, even though it removes them from the Pipfile.lock

**clean**: runs **lock**, then uninstalls packages not specified in Pipfile.lock. (The help text only says that it does the latter, so the fact that it runs **lock** first comes as a surprise. In other words, based on the help text, one thinks it's analogous to **sync**, but it turns out to be analogous to **update**, and there is no analog to **sync**.) It doesn't install packages, even though it adds them to the Pipfile.lock.

In summary: The **sync** command doesn't entirely synchronize anything, which means it's misnamed. And it's weird to have a command that modifies both the Pipfile.lock and the virtual environment yet leaves them unsynchronized -- which is the case for both **update** and **clean**.

----
Given the existing commands, I think that you can completely synchronize everything with the Pipfile by running the following sequence of two commands: **clean**, then **sync**.

So this is mainly a request to add a single command that does what that sequence of two commands does.

But also, secondarily, a suggestion to clean up the existing commands and their documentation.

issue_body:
Hello;

I tried updating setuptools to 40.1.0 in the BuildRoot project, only to find a few packages such as
python-cryptography no longer seem to think setuptools is a version high enough to work.
Another package that has the same issue is python-funcsigs as well.

Downgrading to 40.0.0 fixes the issue.  Any idea as to what could be going on?

Thanks!

Following the report in jaraco/backports.functools_lru_cache#9, I went through the [six backports modules](https://github.com/jaraco?utf8=%E2%9C%93&tab=repositories&q=backports&type=&language=) I maintain and updated them accordingly. This effort led to the release of backports.unittest_mock 1.3, the presence of which is [triggering three new test failures](https://travis-ci.org/pypa/setuptools/builds/233265190) on Python 2.6 and pypy. Pinning to backports.unittest_mock 1.2 works around the issue.

`setup.py test -h` still shows `--test-module`, which is replaced by extended `-s` functionality.

https://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html#test-build-package-and-run-a-unittest-suite

#1032 is about documenting `-s` behavior. However, now `--test-suite` naming for a long option is confusing. It would be nice to deprecate it and replace with `--single` test(module|suite|case|test) run.

```

        'max_iterations': 50,  # stop earlier
        'feature.possible_transitions': False
```

I was expecting that the CRF would only learn transitions found in the training data, and not all possible transitions, and indeed when I print the `info.transitions` from the Tagger object it only shows features from the training data. 

But, when I predict tags, I get transitions which are 'invalid', i.e., they never occurred in the training data. I've noticed this, when working with my own dataset that the CRF was predicting sequence of tags like: `B-PER, I-LOC, I-LOC`, although it happens for very few cases.

I wrote a small script based on your tutorial and on the CONLL dataset, to replicate the problem: 

```

import pycrfsuite

from itertools import chain
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

def word2features(sent, i):
    word = sent[i][0]
    postag = sent[i][1]
    features = [
        'bias',
        'word.lower=' + word.lower(),
        'word[-3:]=' + word[-3:],
        'word[-2:]=' + word[-2:],
        'word.isupper=%s' % word.isupper(),
        'word.istitle=%s' % word.istitle(),
        'word.isdigit=%s' % word.isdigit(),
        'postag=' + postag,
        'postag[:2]=' + postag[:2],
    ]
    if i > 0:
        word1 = sent[i-1][0]
        postag1 = sent[i-1][1]
        features.extend([
            '-1:word.lower=' + word1.lower(),
            '-1:word.istitle=%s' % word1.istitle(),
            '-1:word.isupper=%s' % word1.isupper(),
            '-1:postag=' + postag1,
            '-1:postag[:2]=' + postag1[:2],
        ])
    else:
        features.append('BOS')

    if i < len(sent)-1:
        word1 = sent[i+1][0]
        postag1 = sent[i+1][1]
        features.extend([
            '+1:word.lower=' + word1.lower(),
            '+1:word.istitle=%s' % word1.istitle(),
            '+1:word.isupper=%s' % word1.isupper(),
            '+1:postag=' + postag1,
            '+1:postag[:2]=' + postag1[:2],
        ])
    else:
        features.append('EOS')

    return features

def sent2features(sent):
    return [word2features(sent, i) for i in range(len(sent))]

def sent2labels(sent):
    return [label for token, postag, label in sent]

def sent2tokens(sent):
    return [token for token, postag, label in sent]

def print_transitions(trans_features):
    for (label_from, label_to), weight in trans_features:
        print("%-6s -> %-7s %0.6f" % (label_from, label_to, weight))

def main():
    print("Loading data")

    train_sents = list(nltk.corpus.conll2002.iob_sents('esp.train'))
    test_sents = list(nltk.corpus.conll2002.iob_sents('esp.testb'))

    #train_sents = list(nltk.corpus.conll2000.iob_sents('test.txt'))
    #test_sents = list(nltk.corpus.conll2000.iob_sents('train.txt'))

    print("Extracting features")
    X_train = [sent2features(s) for s in train_sents]
    y_train = [sent2labels(s) for s in train_sents]

    X_test = [sent2features(s) for s in test_sents]
    y_test = [sent2labels(s) for s in test_sents]

    print("Training")
    trainer = pycrfsuite.Trainer(verbose=False)
    for xseq, yseq in zip(X_train, y_train):
        trainer.append(xseq, yseq)
    trainer.set_params({
        'c1': 1.0,   # coefficient for L1 penalty
        'c2': 1e-3,  # coefficient for L2 penalty
        'max_iterations': 50,  # stop earlier
        'feature.possible_transitions': False,
    })
    # save model
    trainer.train('learned-model.crfsuite')

    # open model and predict
    tagger = pycrfsuite.Tagger()
    tagger.open('learned-model.crfsuite')

    y_pred = [tagger.tag(xseq) for xseq in X_test]
    info = tagger.info()

    print("\nTransitions learned:")
    transitions_learned = set()
    for k in Counter(info.transitions):
        transitions_learned.add(k)
    print_transitions(Counter(info.transitions).most_common())

    print("\nTransitions predicted")
    counts = defaultdict(int)
    for i in range(len(y_pred)):
       for tag1, tag2 in zip(y_pred[i], y_pred[i][1:]):
         counts[(tag1,tag2)] += 1

    transitions_predicted = set()
    for k in Counter(counts):
        print(k, counts[k])
        transitions_predicted.add(k)

    print("\nDifference")
    print(transitions_predicted.difference(transitions_learned))

if __name__ == "__main__":

issue_title: A way to structure klein apps into (reusable) modules
issue_body:
**nb. the pattern I'm showing here is basically flask's blueprints**

It seems that currently Klein doesn't have any builtin way to help factoring out view functions to separate modules.

For example, if you had the following app:
```python
app = Klein()

@app.route('/users')
def list_all_users(request):
    return 'all users'
```
then you could divide it into two modules like this:
```python
# main.py
app = Klein()
```
```python
# users.py - version 1
from main import app

@app.route('/users')
# ...
```
This is bad because it leads to several problems, the most immediate being circular import issues, but also this is only a separate module technically, not logically: you can't really re-use that module with another app, or test it in isolation. An easy way to help that would be to use dependency injection:
```python
# main.py
from users import add_users_routes

app = Klein()
add_users_routes(app)
```
```python
# users.py - version 2

def list_all_users(request): 
    return 'all_users'

def add_users_routes(app):
    app.route('/users')(list_all_users)
```
Now `users` is a separate logical module, but it's a bit awkward, with the central `add_users_routes` function. We could use Klein's `.resource()` to help that:
```python
# users.py - version 3
users = Klein()

@users.route('/')
def list_all_users(request):
    return 'all users'

def add_users_routes(app):
    @app.route('/users', branch=True)
    def branch_to_users(request):
        return users.resource()
```
This is already pretty nice, could maybe use some helpers so that you wouldn't need to implement that branch function, but is reusable and possible to test in isolation. The problem however is, routing to a Klein resource returns control to twisted.web, which then calls `.render()` on the nested resource, which causes a werkzeug map bind, etc - it's a performance hit. A simple hello-world-grade benchmark shown that a root Klein can serve ~2200 requests per second, routing one branch deep: ~1700, two branches deep: ~1400 (experience with flask's blueprints shows that 2 levels of nesting are enough in practice)

I'm aware Klein is a web framework, and web applications aren't typically constrained by strict real-time requirements, and they're often easy to scale, but I think Klein needs some guideline on structuring applications, and while we're at it, might as well make it fast :)

Basically I think we want the syntax of `users.py` version 2, with the performance of version 3. Here's how flask does it (translated to Klein):
```python
# main.py
from users import users_blueprint
app = Klein()
users_blueprint.register(app, url_prefix='/users')
```
```python
# users.py
users_blueprint = Blueprint()

@users_blueprint.route('/')
# ...
```
The Blueprint is simply a container that records all `@route()` calls it was used for, and does them on the app when `.register` is called. This makes it only a setup-time thing, with no runtime (performance) effects.

I've put together a proof-of-concept implementation for reference, see https://github.com/tehasdf/klein/tree/blueprints

#!/usr/bin/env python
#

import cyclone.web
from twisted.application import service, internet
from txmongo.connection import ConnectionPool
from twisted.internet import defer
import txmongo


class IndexHandler(cyclone.web.RequestHandler):
    @defer.inlineCallbacks
    def get(self):

        name = self.get_argument("name")
        try:
            result = yield self.settings.db.find({"name": name})
        except Exception, e:
            self.write("find failed: %s\n" % str(e))
        else:
            self.write("result(s): %s\n" % repr(result))
        self.finish()


class WebMongo(cyclone.web.Application):
    def __init__(self):
        handlers = [
            (r"/", IndexHandler),
        ]

        uri = "mongodb://USER:PASSWORD@HOST1:PORT,HOST2:PORT,HOST3:PORT/DB?ssl=true&replicaSet=REPLICA&authSource=admin"
        mongo = ConnectionPool(uri, pool_size=1)

        settings = {
            "db": mongo.test
        }

        cyclone.web.Application.__init__(self, handlers, **settings)


application = service.Application("webmongo")
srv = internet.TCPServer(8888, WebMongo(), interface="127.0.0.1")
srv.setServiceParent(application)

issue_title: Definitions of alpha and gamma in Moffat functions
issue_body:
The documentation in the Moffat1D and Moffat2D models use alpha and gamma, but as far as I can tell, everywhere else these two parameters are defined as beta and alpha, respectively. For example equation 1 in this paper https://arxiv.org/pdf/astro-ph/0109067.pdf

Can we change the code and documentation to reflect this? It has confused me bit while using the models. Thanks.

issue_title: Implement Jarvis March, Graham Scan and Chan's Algorithm
issue_body:
[Chan's Algorithm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chan%27s_algorithm) is a very fast algorithm for computing convex hull of an image. It utilizes [Jarvis March](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gift_wrapping_algorithm) and [Graham Scan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graham_scan) to produce the result in optimal _O_(nlogh) time complexity. I would like to work on its implementation.

For this, I have few questions to ask,

1. Should we make all the three above mentioned algorithms publicly available in the API? Or just the Chan's Algorithm?
2. Should we replace the current implementation of `skimage.morphology.convex_hull_image` with Chan's Algorithm or add a separate function along with it? Or rather use `kwarg` to manage both of them?

Thank you.

issue_title: Add local variables at each backtrace line.
issue_body:
One thing that I frequently find myself wanting is a stack trace that  includes local variables at each stack frame. In Python, all that information is there -- however, it looks like your API doesn't have a place for this information.

I asked Codebase for your API documentation, and they directed me to 
version 2.3, https://help.airbrake.io/kb/api-2/notifier-api-version-23

```
*/notice/error/backtrace/line*

Required. This element can occur more than once. Each line element 
describes one code location or frame in the backtrace when the error 
occurred, and requires @file and @number attributes. If the location 
includes a method or function, the @method attribute should be used. 
```

Notice, there's not really a place to include local variables at each backtrace line. 

@vmihailenco Could we add this into V3? https://help.airbrake.io/kb/api-2/notifier-api-v3

issue_title: Remove Tester class from unit test framework
issue_body: Googletest makes `Tester` obsolete. See discussion starting at https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/pull/179#issuecomment-54864507.

Problem:
`libffi` is used by many languages to include C libraries. You'll probably find `libffi` is not compatible with `nupic.core's` C++ format. Thus you'll not get the portability needed for nupic.core to become ubiquitous. 

Solution:
Make a C wrapper around `nupic.core` which will expose an ABI compatible format.

issue_title: Use full text index for search_names
issue_body:
Using `db_index` for `search_names` is bad way. You should create full text index, not btree.
Currently when importing geo data with many languages I get the error:

```
django.db.utils.OperationalError: index row size 4016 exceeds maximum 2712 for index "geo_city_610a2ab0"
HINT:  Values larger than 1/3 of a buffer page cannot be indexed.
Consider a function index of an MD5 hash of the value, or use full text indexing.
```

issue_title: Error in authorization if no root factory is defined
issue_body:
```
  File "/home/mathieu/Code/Mozilla/readinglist/.venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyramid/config/views.py", line 241, in _permitted
    permission)
  File "/home/mathieu/Code/Mozilla/cliquet/cliquet/authorization.py", line 27, in permits
    if context.prefixed_userid:
AttributeError: DefaultRootFactory instance has no attribute 'prefixed_userid'
```

If the permission system is not used (e.g. readinglist), then it's a pity to query the permission backend. 

Suggestions:
* Add default value for root factory kwarg to ``cliquet.authorization.RouteFactory`` to ``@register`` decorator
* Document clearly that ``cliquet.articles_read_principals = system.Authenticated`` can be used to bypass the permissions backend
* Or otherwise, add a simple fallback like this one in authorization policy:

```python
    def permits(self, context, principals, permission):
        # If no root factory, just required authentication.
        if not isinstance(context, RouteFactory):
            return Authenticated in principals
```

Currently storage methods take a ``cliquet.Resource`` object as first parameter and are not very low level.

I suggest that we refactor/change this to allow low-level create/update/delete of ``records(str, str, json)``.

```python
request.storage.create('__history__', '__internal__', {"action": "click"})
```

```python
request.storage.create('__schema:' + collection, userid, {"title": "form"})
```

We have several possibilities:

1. Rename current methods with a ``resource_`` prefix (or ``_record`` suffix, whatever) and add methods for low-level (get, create, update, delete).
1. Rewrite current backends to perform only low-level operations, and wrap them to provide a resource storage backend like the current one
1. Add another group of backends implementation (e.g. leveldb) (in addition to cache and storage) to provide low-level storage (with a schema similar to cache)
1. Got to go to eat right now.

issue_title: Uniformize the naming of getters in our classes
issue_body:
I've seen that the different contributors to the project don't have the same way to define *getters* on classes.

For instance, on `resource.ViewSet`, I've seen that there are getters prefixed by `get_`, whereas in the *permissions* backend, it's not the case (we have methods named `object_permission_principals` for instance).

In the case of the ViewSet, the design is such that the class can have class attributes (which aren't prefixed by get, because they're attributes) and then getters which start by `get`.

I would advocate we try to follow this pattern everywhere, or update how we do it for viewsets. Thoughts?

issue_title: x509 module with Rest Framework: UNIQUE constraint failed
issue_body:
I have discovered a really strange problem with django-x509. I have created a small demo application to trigger that problem:

https://github.com/hggh/certrest

If you want to create a certificate via the REST API the cert itself is created at the database but the REST Framework throws a IntegrityError.

Steps:

1. git clone https://github.com/hggh/certrest
2. pip install -r requirements.txt
3. ./manage migrate
4. ./mange createsuperuser
5. create a new CA inside the admin: http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin/certs/ca/add/
6. http://127.0.0.1:8000/certs/certs/
7. create a new certificate with the default CreateView in Django: http://127.0.0.1:8000/certs/certs/create/
7.1 this works great the certificate is created and will be shown inside the Listview
8. Goto the REST Framework: http://127.0.0.1:8000/certs/api/cert/
9. create a new certificate within the REST Framework View
10. click on "POST"
11. Django throws: "UNIQUE constraint failed: certs_cert.id"

The library https://github.com/nekobcn/isbnid that we use to normalize an ISBN identifier in the package is not supported anymore.
As you can see here: https://github.com/nekobcn/isbnid/issues, we have opened several issues in the last months, but the author of the package is not replaying.
For this reason we have decide to fork the project and start to maintain it. The new package `isbnid_fork` (https://github.com/inspirehep/isbnid) is already available with pip.

The only thing that should change in `inveniosoftware/idutils` project is this requirement:  https://github.com/inveniosoftware/idutils/blob/33577263914923d117c5e86a1491daf08cbf35b2/setup.py#L26; from `isbnid>=0.3.4'` to `isbnid_fork>=0.4.4`.

issue_title: WebSearch: user-facing exception on search (SPIRES syntax)
issue_body:
Originally on 2012-05-23

When using SPIRES syntax with the query: "find a whatever:", where 'whatever' is followed by a colon and 'whatever' is not a defined index field, there is a using facing exception error displayed:

```
Internal Error exceptions.IndexError list index out of range

URI: https://inspirehep.net/search?ln=en&ln=en&p=find+a+Guillet%3A&of=hb&action_search=Search&sf=&so=d&rm=&rg=25&sc=0
Time: 23/May/2012:11:18:31 +0200
Browser: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/535.19 (KHTML, like Gecko) Ubuntu/12.04 Chromium/18.0.1025.151 Chrome/18.0.1025.151 Safari/535.19
Client: 137.138.124.15
```

I think we should take a look at suppressing this type of error message to something more straightforward or even helping the user to correct his mistake, better yet, correct it automagically. Colons at the end of the search string should probably be stripped/ignored as a general rule.

issue_title: collections: upgrade recipe for tag table
issue_body:
`collections` module relies on `tag` table having:

```
"name": "collection",
"recjson_value": "_collections",
"value": ""
```

Otherwise the `collection` index will not be correct and records not visible.

An upgrade recipe should be provided so that the site admin is alerted.

issue_body:
The following cookiecutter change:

   https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98

should be propagated to this Invenio module.

Namely, in `run-tests.sh`, the sphinx for doctests is invoked after pytest run:

```console
$ tail -3 ./\{\{\ cookiecutter.project_shortname\ \}\}/run-tests.sh
sphinx-build -qnNW docs docs/_build/html && python setup.py test && sphinx-build -qnNW -b doctest docs docs/_build/doctest
```

This sometimes led to problems on Travis CI with the second sphinx-build run due
to "disappearing" dependencies after the example application was tested.

A solution that worked for invenio-marc21 (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49#issuecomment-281287103)
and that was integrated in cookiecutter (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98) was to
run doctest execution in pytest, removing the second `sphinx-build` invocation.

This both solved Travis CI build failures and simplified test suite execution.

Note that this change may necessitate to amend the code tests etc so that things
would be executed with the Flask application context (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49/commits/09e98fc18fd5591dc65d806286e8da51b7e384b4).
PR #119 was merged, but coverage fell simply because ``admin.py`` is not imported. A simple test for the admin interface should be added, to increase the coverage.

- [ ] add missing important API functionality
- [ ] check API function signatures and parameters
- [x] enhance API docstrings (`param`, `returns`, `raises`, `versionadded`)
- [x] plug API functions to existing docs

issue_title: Cannot access static class field from class instance
issue_body:
I recently found out that accessing a static public class field of an instance of a class always returned `None`.
For instance, let's say I have this class:

```
public class MyClass{
     public static final double myStaticClassField = 1;
     public MyClass(){}
}
```

From my Python module, I try this:
```
the_class = autoclass('path.to.MyClass')
print(the_class.myStaticClassField)
>>> 1
instance = the_class()
print(instance.myStaticClassField)
>>> None
```

Did anyone ever encountered this kind of behavior ?
Have a nice day,
S.

```
EnumMap = autoclass('java.util.EnumMap')
SomeEnum = autoclass('my.enum.class')
map = EnumMap(SomeEnum)

fails due to a lack of a suitable ctor.

issue_title: Openssl recipe crashes on x86 arch
issue_body:
p4a branch: stable
buildozer: 0.33
bootstrap: sdl2
kivy: 1.10.0

Error message i get:
```
arm_arch.h:46:6: error: #error "unsupported ARM architecture"
```

When I try to import ssl using a simple import statement, e.g. `import ssl`, I get the error thrown that `_ssl` could not be found. Even when I add `openssl` to the requirements, the import error's still there.

I also tried changing the recipe for hostpython2 (In order to compile with SSL and crypto), but I get errors during the compiling process (Undefined references).

I'm wondering if that's an error of the recipe or a mistake I made

When I exit my app, a strange segmentation error occurs: http://pastebin.com/F8HEaeip

This is with sdl2 and kivy 1.9.2 dev.
It works fine with pygame, what makes me puzzled.

The requirements list: 
`requirements = openssl,geopy,plyer,android,tinydb,hostpython2,kivy==master`

While testing the `RST_editor` example (https://github.com/kivy/kivy/tree/master/examples/RST_Editor) on `HTC Desize Z` I've tried to scroll loaded `rst` file with my trackpad  and got this exception:

```
E/AndroidRuntime(25277): FATAL EXCEPTION: main
E/AndroidRuntime(25277): java.lang.NullPointerException
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at org.renpy.android.SDLSurfaceView.onKeyMultiple(SDLSurfaceView.java:1004)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at org.renpy.android.SDLSurfaceView.onKeyPreIme(SDLSurfaceView.java:1047)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at android.view.View.dispatchKeyEventPreIme(View.java:7179)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at android.view.ViewGroup.dispatchKeyEventPreIme(ViewGroup.java:1338)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at android.view.ViewGroup.dispatchKeyEventPreIme(ViewGroup.java:1338)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at android.view.ViewGroup.dispatchKeyEventPreIme(ViewGroup.java:1338)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at android.view.ViewRootImpl.deliverKeyEvent(ViewRootImpl.java:3608)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at android.view.ViewRootImpl.deliverInputEvent(ViewRootImpl.java:3163)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at android.view.ViewRootImpl.doProcessInputEvents(ViewRootImpl.java:4294)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at android.view.ViewRootImpl$ViewRootHandler.handleMessage(ViewRootImpl.java:2824)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at android.os.Handler.dispatchMessage(Handler.java:99)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at android.os.Looper.loop(Looper.java:137)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at android.app.ActivityThread.main(ActivityThread.java:5041)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at java.lang.reflect.Method.invokeNative(Native Method)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at java.lang.reflect.Method.invoke(Method.java:511)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at com.android.internal.os.ZygoteInit$MethodAndArgsCaller.run(ZygoteInit.java:793)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at com.android.internal.os.ZygoteInit.main(ZygoteInit.java:560)
E/AndroidRuntime(25277):    at dalvik.system.NativeStart.main(Native Method)
```

the culprit line:
https://github.com/kivy/python-for-android/blob/master/src/src/org/renpy/android/SDLSurfaceView.java#L1004

The problem might be that according to http://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/KeyEvent.html#getCharacters() the `trackpad's` event is an `ACTION_MULTIPLE` and `event.getCharacters()` should return `KEYCODE_UNKNOWN` String, instead it returns `null`.
This might be an issue with the `Android NDK` itself, in that case someone more familiar with it could post this bug on a proper tracker.

issue_title: calcuting the distance between matrix and vector - some difference withnumpy
issue_body:
Hello, 
I'm calculating the distance between all `rows` of `matrix m` and some `vector` `v`. m is a large matrix, about 500,000 rows and 2048 column. v is the size of (1,2048)

# Calculation phase:
## numpy
dist_2 = np.sum((nodes - node) ** 2, axis=1)
## numpexpr 
dist_2 = ne.evaluate('sum((nodes - node) ** 2, axis=1)')

# Finding min k phase:
## numpy, numexpr
indx = np.argpartition(dist_2, k)[:k]
distance = dist_2[indx]



First i'm experience some different result between the two ways of calculation 

> vector_size = 2000
> rows_size=500000
> v = np.random.rand(1, vector_size).astype('float32')
> mv = np.random.rand(rows_size, vector_size).astype('float32')
> m = np.asmatrix(mv)

the index of both calcution are the same but the distance can be different start from the `4` digit after the `dot`

for example: 

> indx_numpy__ =    [157516   4720]
> indx_numexpr = [157516   4720]
> distance_k_numpy__ = [ 302.80163574  307.67181396]
> distance_k_numexpr = [ 302.80172729  307.67221069]


Note that above is when using `float32` but when I used `float64` the distance seems to be equal on a debugger but the `logical ==` returned `false`


# Questions:

1. Any idea what there is a different between the distances ?
2. Any idea how to speed up? on this kind of metrics it takes on average 3.5 seconds with numpy and 2.27 seconds with numexpr. My goal is to get it under a second

issue_title: Python 3.2 coverage has a syntax error
issue_body:
which again causes Travis CI tests to fail (#176)

The root cause seems to be that the latest version of coverage (4.1b2) has a syntax error
https://github.com/nedbat/coveragepy/blob/master/coverage/annotate.py#L85 

introduced by adding utf8 support here
https://github.com/nedbat/coveragepy/commit/fdfdb2432dcf9e544e7c6a70421e74e5c56adf45

The standard action seems to be to use coverage 3.7.1 on Python 3.2 or drop Python 3.2 testing (see https://github.com/menegazzo/travispy/issues/20)

issue_title: Erroneous warnings produced since #500 (Function argument not defined in specification)
issue_body:
### Description
It looks like #500 introduced a bug which reports that the function arguments weren't defined when they in fact were.

### Expected behaviour
Function arguments with proper definitions should not produce warnings.


### Actual behaviour
Function arguments with proper definitions do produce warnings.


### Steps to reproduce
Consider the following swagger spec:
```yaml
swagger: "2.0"
basePath: "/v1"
schemes:
- "https"
paths:
  /photo:
    post:
      summary: "Upload a photo along with its metadata"
      description: ""
      operationId: "upload.upload_photo"
      consumes:
      - "multipart/form-data"
      produces:
      - "application/json"
      parameters:
      - name: "photo"
        required: true
        in: "formData"
        type: "file"
        description: "The photo file"
      - name: "imageId"
        required: true
        in: "formData"
        type: "string"
        description: "The UUID assigned to this photo"
      - name: "associations"
        required: true
        in: "formData"
        type: "array"
        collectionFormat: "csv"
        items:
          type: "string"
        description: "A CSV list of QR IDs associated with the photo"
      - name: "takenAt"
        required: true
        in: "formData"
        type: "string"
        description: "ISO 8601 compliant datetime stamp representing when the photo was taken"
      - name: "latitude"
        in: "formData"
        type: "number"
        description: "Latitude portion of where the photo was taken"
      - name: "longitude"
        in: "formData"
        type: "number"
        description: "Longitude portion of where the photo was taken"
      responses:
        201:
          description: "Photo was uploaded and metadata stored successfully"
        409:
          description: "Photo with that ID already exists"
```

and the following Python code:

```python
import connexion

def upload_photo(photo, imageId, associations, takenAt, latitude, longitude):
    print(locals())

app = connexion.App(__name__, specification_dir="spec")
app.add_api("v1.yml")
app.run(port=8080)
```

When running on version `1.1.14` the command line output simply lists the `locals()` output and a POST:

```
 * Running on http://0.0.0.0:8080/ (Press CTRL+C to quit)
{'latitude': 34234.32432434, 'associations': ['aaaa%2Cbbbb%2Ccccc'], 'takenAt': 'dsfdsfdsfsdffdsf', 'longitude': 324324.4444444, 'photo': <FileStorage: 'someimage.png' ('image/png')>, 'imageId': 'abcdef12345678'}
127.0.0.1 - - [04/Oct/2017 14:35:36] "POST /v1/photo HTTP/1.1" 200 -
```

However, when running on version `1.1.15`:

```
 * Running on http://0.0.0.0:8080/ (Press CTRL+C to quit)
Function argument 'imageId' not defined in specification
Function argument 'latitude' not defined in specification
Function argument 'longitude' not defined in specification
Function argument 'takenAt' not defined in specification
Function argument 'associations' not defined in specification
{'longitude': 324324.4444444, 'takenAt': 'dsfdsfdsfsdffdsf', 'associations': ['aaaa%2Cbbbb%2Ccccc'], 'imageId': 'abcdef12345678', 'latitude': 34234.32432434, 'photo': <FileStorage: 'someimage.png' ('image/png')>}
127.0.0.1 - - [04/Oct/2017 14:36:19] "POST /v1/photo HTTP/1.1" 200 -
```

### Additional info:

Output of the commands:

- `python --version` - `Python 3.5.2`
- `pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"` - `Version 1.1.15`

As a side note - the `associations` parameter doesn't seem to get split properly. Am I doing something wrong?

issue_title: Calcul de la PPA - problème avec les dates d'entrée en vigueur
issue_body:
La PPA est calculée en fonction des revenus du trimestre précédent.  Le montant touché au mois N est en fait la moyenne de ce qui aurait pu être perçu par l'agent durant les 3 derniers mois en fonction de ses conditions d'éligibilité pendant cette période.
Pour cela, les dates d'entrée en vigueur des paramètres openfisca ont été antidaté de 3 mois afin de calculer la variable "ppa_fictive" et ainsi, permettre le calcul de la PPA dès le 1er janvier 2016.
Dans les barèmes IPP, nous n'avons pas cette possibilité d'action sur les dates, ce qui bloque le processus de fusion des paramètres IPP/openfisca. 
Les deux solutions pour l'instant envisagées sont : 
- séparer le calcul par des _if_ en faisant appel aux paramètres du "RSA activité" avant le début du 2e trimestre de 2016 et ensuite à ceux de la PPA.
 - séparer le calcul par des _if_ en jouant sur la période à l'aide des "simulation.legislation_at(period.start)" en avançant de 3mois la date de début.
@benjello

There's a setting that can be [set on CircleCI](https://circleci.com/docs/2.0/skip-build/#auto-cancelling-a-redundant-build) to automatically cancel redundant builds (eg pushing several times to the same branch, before the previous builds were finished).

This might (or might no) be desirable. It saves some CPU power (and thus saves the planet and polar bears). The drawbacks seem minimal (not having a complete build on a previous version of the branch).

i.update(fields={'assignee':{'name':'foo'}})

Part of: https://github.com/clab/dynet/issues/1276

We have a description of Minibatching here:
> http://dynet.readthedocs.io/en/latest/minibatch.html

But I think this should be moved to the tutorial and (1) add Python and C++ examples, (2) add these to the examples directory as well.

This command

```
produces a different result between Mac and Windows, and even between different Mac environments — the expected result was generated on a MBP running macOS 10.13.6, and it's failing on the Travis CI.

now there is a mixture of script that support both py2 as py3 others aren't upgraded yet

are there any plans todo so?

issue_title: OAuth signature does not match with wordpress API
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm trying to perform a post request to wp api, but I get the error "signature does not match". The same request, sent through Postman, works.

The code

```
TOKEN = u'...'
URL = u'...'
CLIENT_KEY = u'...'
CLIENT_SECRET = u'...'
TOKEN = u'...'
TOKEN_SECRET = u'...'

def main():

    post = {
        'title': 'Test python',
        'content': 'message python',
        'status': 'publish'
    }
    
    oauth = OAuth1Session(CLIENT_KEY,
                   CLIENT_SECRET,
                   TOKEN,
                   TOKEN_SECRET,
                   signature_type='auth_header')
    
    r = oauth.post(URL + 'posts/', data=post)

```

Thanks!

issue_title: gcloud-python 0.9.0 SSL broken in app engine
issue_body:
I noticed the depending crypto module was changed since commit 948cccf3. The project was deployed with GAE release 1.9.32 and gcloud-python 0.9.0.

Here is some context info according to your recent comments:

1. How do you vendor in gcloud-python for App Engine?
As described in GAE documentation: https://cloud.google.com/appengine/docs/python/tools/libraries27#vendoring

2. Which services / gcloud-python subpackages do you use? (I'm curious which imports / import paths your code has been using.)
I am generating signed url for an object in Google Cloud Storage. Here's the partial stacktrace:
> Traceback (most recent call last):
> ---- omitted for simple trace) ----
>     from ext import cloudstorage as storage
>   File "/base/data/home/apps/s~jackend-0/1.390622100755930629/ext/cloudstorage.py", line 8, in <module>
>     from gcloud import (
>   File "/base/data/home/apps/s~jackend-0/1.390622100755930629/lib/gcloud/storage/__init__.py", line 43, in <module>
>     from gcloud.storage.blob import Blob
>   File "/base/data/home/apps/s~jackend-0/1.390622100755930629/lib/gcloud/storage/blob.py", line 28, in <module>
>     from gcloud.credentials import generate_signed_url
>   File "/base/data/home/apps/s~jackend-0/1.390622100755930629/lib/gcloud/credentials.py", line 22, in <module>
>     from OpenSSL import crypto
>   File "/base/data/home/apps/s~jackend-0/1.390622100755930629/lib/OpenSSL/__init__.py", line 8, in <module>
>     from OpenSSL import rand, crypto, SSL
>   File "/base/data/home/apps/s~jackend-0/1.390622100755930629/lib/OpenSSL/rand.py", line 11, in <module>
>     from OpenSSL._util import (
>   File "/base/data/home/apps/s~jackend-0/1.390622100755930629/lib/OpenSSL/_util.py", line 6, in <module>
>     from cryptography.hazmat.bindings.openssl.binding import Binding
>   File "/base/data/home/apps/s~jackend-0/1.390622100755930629/lib/cryptography/hazmat/bindings/openssl/binding.py", line 14, in <module>
>     from cryptography.hazmat.bindings._openssl import ffi, lib
> ImportError: dynamic module does not define init function (init_openssl)


I guess this is caused by the inherent inflexibility with runtime environment in GAE, and affects #1009 which seems to be conflict with this issue.

issue_title: Dataset isn't in the docs as a "concept"
issue_body:
We added back the Dataset class (basically an alias class that fills in some blanks for you), but it's not listed on the left navigation of the docs. Any chance we can put that back?

If we do, we should add a lovely `..note:` banner on that page saying that if you only interact with one dataset (a default one) that you don't need to worry about this, and you can use `datastore.<method>` and we'll automatically use the default dataset.

issue_title: Solicit some dev exp feedback about config / auth
Storage: there is no way to remove labels from a bucket
'_AsyncQuery.cancel' fails to update from returned resource
issue_body:
E.g.:

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ...
    job.cancel()
  File ".../google/cloud/bigquery/job.py", line 378, in cancel
    self._set_properties(api_response)
  File ".../google/cloud/bigquery/job.py", line 262, in _set_properties
    self._scrub_local_properties(cleaned)
  File ".../google/cloud/bigquery/job.py", line 1050, in _scrub_local_properties
    configuration = cleaned['configuration']['query']
KeyError: 'configuration'
```

The [docs for `job.cancel`](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/v2/jobs/cancel#response) show that the job resource is in a `job` subkey of the response.

issue_title: Should language document_from_text() etc. be a reference to the Document class
issue_body:
See https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/pull/2062/files/d36ad4db94666450786d4f667aea0607ff2113cb#r76032786

The question is whether we should have `Client.document_from_foo` methods or just have `Client.document` be a reference to the class `Document` and then use `Client.document.from_foo`.

This amounts to swapping `_` for `.` (i.e. no less typing) and makes a "proper noun" like `Document` be referenced as an "improper noun" as a property, which is not a win IMO

- [ ] [IAM](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/895951a7ae244634f44debee27597ef384b6c138/core/google/cloud/iam.py) (Related #3882)
- [ ] [Helpers](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/895951a7ae244634f44debee27597ef384b6c138/core/google/cloud/_helpers.py) - many of these will just be distributed among the existing helper modules in api_core.
- [ ] [Connection/JSONConnection](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/4c24da38f1a8d9a3cc6422cf0c20495880d1fe38/core/google/cloud/_http.py)
- [ ] [Client base](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/4c24da38f1a8d9a3cc6422cf0c20495880d1fe38/core/google/cloud/client.py) - (Consider eliminating in favor of factory mixins and helpers?)

issue_title: retrieved token does not grant access to related resource (401 Error)
issue_body:
Hi, 
we stumbled over what we think can be categorized as a bug in the library. We are able to successfully obtain a token for a given resource and client id. However, the token does not grant us access to the resource. Whenever we call the resource with the `access_token`, we get a HTTP 401 response.

When we use the .Net library, with the exact same parameters, the token works.

The difference we have found is that the python library explicitly sends the parameter `api-version=1.0` to the oauth2 endpoint.

`POST https://login.microsoftonline.com/common/oauth2/token?api-version=1.0`

Once we changed the following line in `oauth2_client.py` in the adal library, I could access my resource.

`return urlparse('{}?{}'.format(self._token_endpoint, urlencode(parameters)))`

in the method `_create_token_url`, to

`return urlparse(self._token_endpoint)`.

See also this question on StackOverflow, the latest answer is from us.
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/37909332/401-unauthorized-making-rest-call-to-azure-api-app-using-bearer-token/

issue_title: EC2 NoDevice error with boolean values.
issue_body:
It appears that the `NoDevice` field in the `BlockDeviceMapping` was updated in #858 to be a boolean.  Unfortunately now, the generated templates are failing to deploy, regardless of what value you use for the field.  When set to either True or False, I receive the following error when trying to deploy a generated template via CloudFormation: `Value of property NoDevice must be an object`

Looking at the [docs](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-ec2-blockdev-mapping.html#cfn-ec2-blockdev-mapping-nodevice)  the type is listed as boolean, however the last example on the page `Unmapping an AMI-defined Device` shows the use of an empty map, `{}`, and not a string or boolean value.

I saw that @tweakster noted he received a error stating this should be a string, but I have tried multiple regions, and always receive the same error if an empty map is not used.

We have been using this value successfully in previous versions, and I propose that the change be reverted.  I will work on submitting the required code change, later today, and can provide a sample of working code using this empty mapping as well.

issue_title: ECR Repository does not accept LifecyclePolicy object as a value for LifecyclePolicy parameter.
issue_body:
The property was added on https://github.com/cloudtools/troposphere/commit/43ff5d3fe1ff59d01def4522a136b5eb1c64b8ee though when I try to add a lifecycle policy to my repository, using the LifecyclePolicy I get a error telling troposphere expects a basestring instead of that object.

And when using a string I got the following error message from Cloudformation: "Value of property LifecyclePolicy must be an object"

I've made some minor tweaks in order for it to work and will open a PR for it. :)

This is on OSX against `pex 1.4.2` using `python 2.7.13`.
Same command works against `pex 1.3.2`

Command:
```
$ pex -r requirements.txt -o env.pex
```

Contents of `requirements.txt`:
```
# === Main Dependencies
flask>=0.0
pathlib2
dulwich
pandas
codenamize
pyOpenSSL>=16.2.0
jupyter
```

When attempting to run `env.pex`:

```
$ ./env.pex
Failed to execute PEX file, missing macosx_10_12_x86_64-cp-27-cp27m compatible dependencies for:
ipython
appnope
```

Interestingly, changing the `requirements.txt` to include `appnope` and `ipython==5.7` explicitly fixes it against `pex 1.4.2`.

There were some issues raised during review that were marked Done but appear to be still live. @stuhood intends to take another pass so this is to track them.

- It is skip=False by default
       * Scala consumers first interaction with Pants will likely be an error that asks to reformat their source code.
-  Operates over every Target in self.context.targets().
       * Should limit itself to scala_library and tests.
- Uses goal based scheduling instead of using Products.
       * The SimpleCodegen products model is probably a more flexible choice.
       * What if someone wants another compile task to go first?
        * I don't think scalafmt warrants hardcoded priority, it doesn't actually need anything besides to run before `compile.zinc`
- There is a default config file set in the task, but I do not believe that config file is being made available in the sdist.
- Upon failure, it prints the full (adjusted?) text of one file to terminal
- It suggests running

        `FAILURE: Scalafmt failed with exit code 899 to fix run: `scalafmt -i --files ${files}`

where the `${files[@]}` are every scala file it ran over, whether it passed or not.

* There is no config file included in the suggested output, so the "fix" run may use a different config than the check config.

- It bootstraps its own scalafmt tool, but suggests using the system scalafmt.
       * It could at least point at the bootstrapped tool (and include the path to the config file).
       * Our engineers do not currently have a system `scalafmt`, for example
- A nice-to-have would be to surface the scalafmt tool as an option, like the test.junit does for the runner.

issue_title: UX issue: setup not friendly enough for python only project
issue_body:
I have a box that has no jdk installed, and the project is purely python.

Since the default pants.ini is empty based on http://www.pantsbuild.org/install.html

```
$ ./pants binary src/python/main

19:18:32 00:00 [main]
               (To run a reporting server: ./pants server)
19:18:32 00:00   [setup]
19:18:32 00:00     [parse]
               Executing tasks in goals: bootstrap -> imports -> unpack-jars -> jvm-platform-validate -> deferred-sources -> gen -> resolve -> resources -> compile -> binary
19:18:32 00:00   [bootstrap]

FAILURE: Problem locating a java distribution: Failed to locate a JRE distribution with minimum_version None, maximum_version None


19:18:32 00:00   [complete]
               FAILURE
```

Sending `Ctrl+D` to a scala repl usually triggers shutdown of the repl, but in the context of the daemon, it does not. `stdin` properly closes (ie, the `ChunkType.STDIN_EOF` message is properly sent), but some other magic is missing that is preventing stdin from completely closing.

On the other hand, something like:
```
echo 'println("hello!")' | ./pants --pants-config-files=pants.daemon.ini repl src/scala/org/pantsbuild/zinc/analysis
```
...works fine and exits cleanly. Also, sending `Ctrl+C` does what you'd expect and cleanly exits the repl.

Borrowing @baroquebobcat 's idea, sometimes it is good to have a quick peek on the target declaration, because sometimes a BUILD can contain multiple target declarations, so the `peek` goal will just show 

Example:
In `foo/bar/BUILD` file:
```
target(name = 'a',
  dependencies=['other:targets', 'that:might', 'be/useful:to-show']
)

target(name = 'b',
  dependencies=['other:targets', 'that:might', 'be/useful:to-show']
)

target(name = 'c',
  dependencies=['other:targets', 'that:might', 'be/useful:to-show']
)
```
Result
```
./pants peek foo/bar:a
# target/to/BUILD:1 // line number
target(name = 'a',
  dependencies=['other:targets', 'that:might', 'be/useful:to-show']
)
```

issue_title: sbt name hashing breaks jvm dependency analysis
issue_body: Enabling name hashing breaks the dep-analyzer and dep-usage tasks, which parse dependencies from a particular portion of the analysis that is not populated with name hashing enabled. Instead, name hashing causes other sections to be filled with similar information.

issue_body:
Dx can take Zip-compatible archives as args. So unless we need
to filter the file list, we do not need to unpack the jars themselves.

Pass the classes.jar to javac, as it already stands. No special flags or 
handling is required for the dx tool, it processes .class files, dirs or archives
opaquely. The unpack_libraries product is currently the directory that
holds the entire source of the unpack jar, optionally passing the jar
file as the product seems like it would be fine.

issue_title: Output zipkin traces for remote execution
issue_body:
Profiling and debugging remote execution is currently hard; we have some logging, but nothing structured.

Let's modify the `process_execution::remote::CommandRunner` so that it tracks timings of key events, and outputs a zipkin trace for the execution.

Key events:
* Making the `execute` RPC (and getting a final)
* Starting and finishing uploading any files/digests that may be needed, both per-digest and for batches

I'm imagining a new struct along the lines of:
```
struct TimingEvent {
  execution_id: String, // just a uuid
  description: String, // something like "Uploading digest abc123 for execution of action with digest def456" or "Waiting for action with digest def456 to complete"
  start_time: std::time::SystemTime,
  finish_time: std::time::SystemTime,
}
```
the `remote::CommandRunner` having a `Vec` of these things, and the `run` method passing around an incomplete one, then adding it to the `Vec` when it's complete.

We can punt on formatting these as zipkin traces in the short term - just dumping these as JSON is better than nothing, but let's try to stay as close as possible to zipkin so that it's easy to zipkin-ise these in the future.
Can we get a new release? For some reason, pip does not want to install DSE from the repo, which means we can't use it at all unless the <=1.9 requirement is gone.

issue_title: Can't package swagger templates using current flow
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm trying to package a swagger spec using the flow proposed by SAM, but it doesn't do the transform properly.

When specifying an `AWS::Serverless:Api` resource, I have two options for the swagger spec:

1. `DefinitionUri`
2. `DefinitionBody`

When I use `DefinitionUri`, then any references to Lambda functions in my swagger document will not get transformed to the actual ARNs of the Lambda functions, e.g. (in swagger.yaml)

```
[snip snip]
      x-amazon-apigateway-integration:
        responses:
          default:
            statusCode: 200
        uri: 
          Fn::Sub: "arn:aws:apigateway:${AWS::Region}:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/${LambdaFunction.Arn}/invocations"
[snip snip]
```

According to the swagger CORS example in the SAM examples folder, you use `DefinitionBody`. The problem with using `DefinitionBody` is that the `aws cloudformation package` command does not transform any references to a local swagger file into a remote file in S3.

The workaround is that I actually put the swagger spec in S3 myself, but this is clunky and contrary to the existing model.

issue_title: Can't retrieve lambda policy for function created by SAM
issue_body: Hi, I have a simple Lambda function connected to API Gateway, similar to the swagger cors example. I'm running into a similar issue as in https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/issues/59 , so I'm trying to debug what is the problem with the `AWS::Lambda::Permission`. However, when I run `aws lambda get-policy --function-name `, I get this response: ``` › aws lambda get-policy --function-name xxxxxx-URUCPO8SEL49 An error occurred (ResourceNotFoundException) when calling the GetPolicy operation: The resource you requested does not exist. ``` Querying any of my other Lambda functions, I get the expected policy in return. Not being able to query the policy makes this difficult to debug. I do have the usual 2 permissions associated with the lambda function: Using the same workaround as suggested in https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/issues/59, I manage to get a hint to the nature of the problem. I added my own `AWS::Lambda::Permission` resource to the SAM template as follows: ``` SomePermission: Type: AWS::Lambda::Permission DependsOn: - API - CreateUpload Properties: Action: lambda:InvokeFunction FunctionName: !Ref SomeLambda Principal: apigateway.amazonaws.com ``` Now all of a sudden running `get-policy` gives me a response, and I am also able to successfully invoke the API. Seems like there is a problem with the `AWS::Lambda::Permission`s that are setup by SAM, and the only way to fix it is to create a third Permission. there is a problem in
CT_CoreProperties._set_element_text (line 299)

```
value = str(value)
```

which will will fail in a default windows-python2.x installation when supplying unicode strings (e.g.):

> UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\xfc' in position 0: ordinal not in range(128)

This is because there is no default system encoding, so python tries to encode the unicode-string using 7-bit-ASCII, which of course fails.

My solution: Only convert to string, if its necessary. If it's already a string/unicode, don't touch.

```
if not isinstance(value, basestring):
    value = str(value)
```

issue_title: Add user-friendly documentation for address tagging
issue_body:
The current documentation isn't very helpful for quickly understanding the US Thoroughfare, Landmark, and Postal Address Data Standard. We ask users to either read [the official docs](http://www.urisa.org/clientuploads/directory/GMI/Professional%20Practice/Address%20Standard/AddressStandard_Approved_Apr11_03Class.pdf) (which is long and known to induce headaches) or [see examples in our docs](http://usaddress.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#details) (which don't cover the most confusing edge cases, and also aren't very well advertised). To encourage users to tag their own training data, we should improve the information on our documentation page and make sure it's better advertised.

We should also add a short note about what to do if tests fail in the process of making a new build.

issue_title: web terminal: typing doesn't work after Ctrl-A or Ctrl-E
issue_body: In the web terminal, after typing ctrl-a or ctrl-e to go to the beginning or end of the current command, hitting any key is a no-op, though after that, you can type again.

issue_title: out of memory while retrieving all objects in a table
issue_body:
I have about 700 thousand objects in a table and i want to retrieve all of them.
So, i do this:
```python
all_objects = Person.objects.limit(1000000).all()
for item in all_objects:
    #do something
```
Here come the problems.
1.  it took a little time to execute the first line and it's not a big problem. 
2.  the little program ate about 4g of my memory and finally killed by the system.

I went through the source code and i found two related methods in class AbstractQuerySet :
*  
```python
    def _execute_query(self):
        if self._batch:
            raise CQLEngineException("Only inserts, updates, and deletes are available in batch mode")
        if self._result_cache is None:
            self._result_cache = list(self._execute(self._select_query()))
            self._construct_result = self._get_result_constructor()
```
so, you cached all the 700 thousand objects? 

*  
```python
    def __iter__(self):
        self._execute_query()

        for idx in range(len(self._result_cache)):
            instance = self._result_cache[idx]
            if isinstance(instance, dict):
                self._fill_result_cache_to_idx(idx)
            yield self._result_cache[idx]
```
1. i think it's better to use range in six.moves ...
2. the usage of memory kept increasing while iterating, i think it may have something to do with the `_fill_result_cache_to_idx`

issue_title: DIAMOND output has qstart > qend when frame < 0
issue_body: When DIAMOND blastx finds a match in a reverse complemented read, the qstart and qend numbers it emits are decreasing. I.e., they are indices into the read from its end, not its beginning. This is a difference from BLAST, which always has qstart < qend. Our DIAMOND hsp.py normalization code needs to take this into account.

issue_title: Note in docs that several of these features are available in newer Python
issue_body:
I was using Monty because it was included with pymatgen. However, I think it is important to note that some features of Monty are available in Python >=  2.6 with a slightly different api. I understand that this package is compatible with Python 2 and 3 which is currently an advantage. Especially in the scientific community.

For example:

 - Starting in 3.2, there is support for transparent archiving and unarchiving of files via `shutil`. This does much of `monty.shutil`.
 - Starting in 3.2, there is support for temporary directories using the with syntax. `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory`.
- Starting in 2.6, there is support for number of cpus `multiprocessing.cpu_count()`

issue_title: How to share session between saspy and cell magics?
issue_body:
This library is enormously useful for cases when I want to write in Python but switch to SAS for certain heavy lifting segments -- but I'm running into one workflow issue when attempting to combine the object-based saspy methods with the cell magics approach.

My goal is to do something like this, in a series of cells in one notebook:

```python
# start a session
import saspy
import pandas as pd
from IPython.display import HTML

sas = saspy.SASsession(cfgname='ssh')
```

 Later, load something into the SAS session:

```python
sas_dataset = sas.df2sd(something_in_pandas, 'mydata')
```

Now, instantiate cell magics so that I can submit SAS statements directly:

```python
%load_ext saspy.sas_magic
```

Do something useful in SAS (hello world example here):

```python
%%SAS

proc print data=WORK.mydata; run;
```

But the issue is that the cell magics extension, when loaded, creates a new SAS session on the other end, separate from the one created with the direct `saspy.SASsession` call, which means that my work library and other instantiated data are not available to me in any %SAS cells.

I can of course get around this by using the more explicit syntax:

```python
c = sas.submit("""
  ... sas code here
""")
HTML(c['LST'])
```

But that isn't quite as elegant for a single notebook trading off tasks between python and SAS. Is there a way to force a shared session between both, or a reason to prevent that?  

Ideally, an existing session could simply be passed through into the magics when loaded. This line is where the new session is created:

https://github.com/sassoftware/saspy/blob/master/saspy/sas_magic.py#L36

Although as far as I can tell from API docs, the `load_ext` call in iPython doesn't allow arguments, so I'm not sure how passing in an already instantiated SAS session would be done in this case.

issue_title: Advertised VPN prefix not correctly received on remote device
issue_body:
<!---
Verify first that your issue/request is not already reported on GitHub.
Also test if the latest release, and master branch are affected too.
-->

##### ISSUE TYPE
<!--- Pick one below and delete the rest: -->
 - Bug Report


##### OS
<!---
Centos7
-->

##### VERSION
<!--- Paste verbatim the output from “exabgp --version” between quotes below -->
```
ExaBGP : 3.4.23
Python : 2.7.5 (default, Aug  4 2017, 00:39:18)  [GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-16)]
Uname  : #1 SMP Tue Sep 12 22:26:13 UTC 2017
```

##### ENVIRONMENT 
<!--- Paste verbatim the output from “exabgp --di” (let us know if the output is empty) between quotes below -->
```
[exabgp.daemon]
user = 'root'

[exabgp.log]
message = true


```

<!--- You can also use gist.github.com links for larger files -->

##### CONFIGURATION
<!--- Paste verbatim your configuration file
```
neighbor 192.168.1.1 {
  router-id 10.0.0.10;
  local-address 10.0.0.10;
  local-as 65534;
  peer-as 65534;
  hold-time 90;
  family {
    ipv4 flow-vpn;
    ipv4 mpls-vpn;
  }
      process announce {
        run /etc/announce.py; 
      }
}

announce.py generates a route annouce with a 20.20.20.0/24
exabgp: 30032  processes     Command from process announce : announce route 20.20.20.0/24 next-hop 10.20.20.253 rd 10.0.0.10:65002 community 65534:6666 extended-community target:65534:65001

```

<!--- You can also use gist.github.com links for larger files -->

##### SUMMARY
<!--- Explain the problem briefly -->
Wrong prefix seen in advertised IPv4 VPN route.  I'm advertising IPv4 VPN route <any prefix> to my VPN router via the process called announce but instead see 0.0.0.0/0 installed into my Juniper VPN table.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
<!---
For bugs, please if possible, please run with the "-d" option and provide the full output,
If not possible, please provide the full fault section as reported by ExaBGP
-->
